summary: Gruber announces Dithering, a new subscription podcast he's co-hosting with Ben Thompson of Stratechery. The show features three 15-minute episodes per week at $5/month or $50/year, built entirely on open RSS with no proprietary app. He recounts his history with subscription models, from Daring Fireball's failed member-only RSS feeds in 2004 (killed by Google Reader's dominance) to the successful weekly sponsorship model he's used for 13 years. He frames Dithering as his return to the subscription idea he's always believed in, while emphasizing it supplements rather than replaces The Talk Show.
key_points: Dithering is a strict-format podcast: exactly 15 minutes, three times per week, subscription-only at $5/month
Gruber's first subscription experiment in 2004 failed because member-only RSS feeds were incompatible with Google Reader's dominance
Despite producing millions of words and hundreds of podcast hours for free, Gruber has always believed in subscription-supported independent media
The show is built on open RSS standards with no proprietary app, working with any podcast player
Ben Thompson's Stratechery membership infrastructure powers the backend, with bundle discounts available
money_quotes: Working title for my autobiography: This Took a Lot Longer Than I Thought It Would.
Ben, in particular, has long been convinced — correctly, I obviously believe — that paid subscriptions are a natural fit for podcasts, but yet have been almost entirely untried.
Episodes of The Talk Show are — depending on your personal taste and available time — either famously or infamously long.
Rather than fight the tide, I went with the tide, and made the full content feed free for everyone and decided to try monetizing it through weekly sponsorships instead of subscriptions.
Not one word, not one minute behind a subscriber paywall.
But I love and believe in the idea of reader / listener / member-supported subscriptions, and I've been looking for something I could offer that feels right. That's Dithering.
From a medium-is-the-message perspective, I think we're doing something neat with Dithering. Episodes exist only in the feed, and thus, from a listener's perspective, only in their podcast player.
The Dithering branding brings warmth to my cold heart.
key_insight: After 16 years of experimenting with monetization models for independent media, Gruber returns to the subscription model he always believed in, this time with a tightly constrained podcast format built on open standards.
